question: Why is compost heap?
title: Why Build a Compost Heap? The Biology of Turning Waste Into Living Soil
metaDescription: A compost heap is how you close the loop between what you take from the land and what you give back. Scotty explains the science and the philosophy behind hot composting.
content:
Albert Howard had a phrase he came back to over and over in his work: the law of return. The idea is simple. Whatever you take off the land — through crops, through harvest, through grazing — you have to give back. Not because it's a nice sentiment, but because biology demands it. The decay cycle doesn't stop. It can't. And if you're not consciously participating in that return, you're mining the soil and calling it farming.

A compost heap is how you participate. That's the short answer to why you build one.

The longer answer involves thermophilic bacteria, carbon-to-nitrogen ratios, the steam that comes off a hot pile on a cold morning, and what it actually feels like to turn a two-week-old pile and watch it alive with decomposers. But the core of it is this: a compost heap is a managed version of what the forest floor does naturally. You are concentrating the decay cycle, accelerating it, and capturing the result to feed your soil.

Let me walk you through how it works and why it matters.

---

## What a Compost Heap Actually Is

A compost heap is a pile of organic material — food scraps, yard waste, leaves, manure, wood chips — assembled in proportions that allow the right microbial communities to take over, generate heat, and break everything down into a stable, nutrient-dense material called humus.

The Cornell Composting science program describes composting as the process that speeds up decomposition of organic materials by providing ideal conditions for microorganisms to thrive. That's the operating principle. You're not doing the work. The microbes are. You're just building them a good home.

The "heap" part matters. Size is not arbitrary. A pile needs mass to hold heat and build the internal temperature that thermophilic bacteria require to really get going. A pile that's too small dissipates its heat too quickly and never reaches the active thermophilic phase where the real magic happens. A pile that's too large can go anaerobic in the center — which is when things start smelling bad and the wrong microbial communities take over. The sweet spot is roughly three feet by three feet by three feet as a minimum, up to about five feet in any direction.

## The Biology: What's Happening Inside the Pile

The decomposition process moves through distinct phases, each dominated by different microbial communities.

In the first stage — the mesophilic phase — bacteria that thrive at moderate temperatures (50 to 110 degrees Fahrenheit) move in and begin breaking down the readily available sugars, starches, and simple compounds in your organic matter. This happens fast. Within the first day or two of a well-built pile, you'll notice the pile warming from the inside. The bacteria are metabolizing, generating heat as a byproduct, and the pile temperature begins to climb.

Then the thermophilic bacteria take over. These are organisms built for heat — they prefer temperatures between 104 and 158 degrees Fahrenheit. Research from Cornell's composting program documents that decomposition occurs most rapidly during this thermophilic stage, which can last for weeks depending on pile composition and management. This is when your pile is working hardest. You'll see steam rising when you pitch it. Internal temperatures can reach 140 to 150 degrees Fahrenheit in a well-managed pile — hot enough to kill most weed seeds and pathogens.

After the thermophilic phase, as the readily available carbon is used up, the pile cools. The mesophiles return. Fungi move in. Actinomycetes — those white, thread-like organisms you'll see when you dig into a maturing pile — begin breaking down the tougher lignin and cellulose compounds that bacteria can't easily reach. Finally, worms and macro-invertebrates move in to do the last stage of processing, and what you're left with is dark, crumbly, biologically active compost.

## Why Carbon and Nitrogen Both Matter

You've probably heard about the carbon-to-nitrogen ratio. It's one of those things people overcomplicate, but the concept is straightforward.

Bacteria need both carbon for energy and nitrogen for building protein. When you pile up too much carbon-heavy material — dry leaves, straw, wood chips — without enough nitrogen, the pile is slow and cold because there isn't enough nitrogen for the bacteria to multiply rapidly. When you pile up too much nitrogen-heavy material — fresh grass clippings, food scraps, manure — without enough carbon, the pile goes anaerobic and starts producing ammonia, which is that sharp smell that tells you something's wrong.

The sweet spot is a ratio of roughly 25 to 30 parts carbon to 1 part nitrogen by weight. In practice, this means layering: a layer of greens (your food scraps, fresh plant material, anything wet and nitrogen-rich), then a layer of browns (dry leaves, straw, shredded paper). What I do is what I call lasagna layering — stack the rotten vegetables from kitchen runs with a carbon layer on top, and repeat. That structure gives the bacteria what they need and keeps the pile aerobic.

## The Law of Return in Practice

Here's what I want you to think about. Everything you take from a garden bed — every lettuce head, every tomato, every pound of greens you harvest — represents nutrients that were in that soil. Nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, calcium, magnesium, trace minerals. They're now in the food. When you eat the food and compost your kitchen scraps, you're beginning the return of those nutrients. When you put that finished compost back on the garden bed, the return is complete.

This is what Gabe Brown talks about when he describes the cycle of biology. The nutrients aren't destroyed — they're just moving. Plants capture them from soil, animals eat plants, decomposers break down the residues, and soil microbes make the nutrients plant-available again. A compost heap is your deliberate participation in that cycle.

Without it, you're mining. You're taking nutrients out of the ground, shipping them to your table, and sending them to the landfill when you throw away your food scraps. The cycle is broken. The soil gets poorer. You add synthetic fertilizer to compensate, and that fertilizer — being soluble — runs off in the first heavy rain and ends up in the watershed instead of feeding your plants. It's an expensive, ecologically destructive way to manage the land.

A compost heap closes the loop. It's not complicated. It's not expensive. It's the oldest fertility management system on earth.

## Compost and the Living Soil Connection

Here is the thing I want you to understand about finished compost that most people miss. It is not just fertilizer. It is not a bag of nutrients. It is a living inoculant.

When you add finished compost to a depleted garden bed, you are not just adding nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium. You are adding billions of bacteria, fungi, nematodes, protozoa, and other organisms that establish themselves in the soil and begin running the soil food web. The bacteria colonize mineral particles and begin releasing locked-up nutrients. The mycorrhizal fungi form networks that extend the reach of plant roots. The predatory organisms — nematodes, protozoa — prey on bacteria and release nutrients in plant-available forms through their excretions.

This is what Albert Howard observed in the Indore composting method he developed in India in the 1930s. He was not just making fertilizer. He was cultivating a biological community and transferring that community to depleted agricultural soils. The crops grown in Indore-composted soil were not just better fed — they were disease-resistant, drought-tolerant, and biologically vital in ways that synthetic fertilizer could not replicate.

Gabe Brown makes this same argument from a different angle — that the soil biology is the infrastructure, and that the farmer's job is to protect and feed that infrastructure, not bypass it with soluble chemical inputs. A compost heap is how you build the infrastructure. It is how you run the biology on your own terms, in your own backyard, with inputs that would otherwise go to a landfill.

## What You Need to Build One

Start with your food scraps and whatever organic matter you have access to. Fruit and vegetable trimmings, coffee grounds, eggshells — all of that is gold. Get your carbon source lined up — dry leaves are the easiest and most abundant, and if you don't have them at your house, your neighbors probably do in the fall. Layer them.

Water the pile if it's too dry — it should feel like a wrung-out sponge, moist but not dripping. Pitch it — turn it with a pitchfork — at least once a week to introduce oxygen. Aerobic bacteria need oxygen levels above five percent to function. When a pile goes anaerobic, the wrong microbes take over and the whole process bogs down.

Don't add meat, bones, or dairy to a backyard pile. Not because they can't compost — they can — but because they attract pests and create odor problems in a residential setting. Stick to plant material and you'll have a neighbor-friendly pile that does exactly what you want it to do.

In six to eight weeks of active management, you'll have finished compost that smells like rich, earthy forest floor. Put it on your beds. Feed your soil. Complete the cycle.

That's why you build a compost heap.

---

sources.title: The Science of Composting: How Compost Happens

scottyQuotes: Composting is a great way to add organic matter back to the soil.
My pile has gone hot! From here, it will be much more active breaking down.
We lasagna layer carbon and nitrogen — rotten vegetables, then leaves, then repeat.
